mnemonics.title: DFA vs NFA Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: D-eterministic means 'One Way' (unique transition), N-on means 'Many Ways' (multiple possible transitions). Think: DFA = Definite, NFA = Not Sure (many choices).

mnemonics.title: Chomsky Hierarchy Order
mnemonics.content: Remember: Type 0 = Unrestricted (U), Type 1 = Context-sensitive (C), Type 2 = Context-free (F), Type 3 = Regular (R). Alphabetical: U-C-F-R or Numerical: 3-2-1-0 (reverse order of power).

mnemonics.title: P vs NP Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: P = 'Polynomial time to Solve' (Find solution), NP = 'Non-deterministic Polynomial time to Verify' (Check solution). Think: P = Problem Solver, NP = Proof Checker.

mnemonics.title: Closure Properties
mnemonics.content: Regular: Union, Intersection, Complement, Concatenation, Star all closed. CFL: Union, Concatenation, Star closed; Intersection and Complement NOT closed. Remember: RICC (Regular Intersect Closed, CFL Intersect NOT Closed).

mnemonics.title: NP-Complete Meaning
mnemonics.content: NPC = 'Not P Completion' or 'New Cook-Levin' (named after Cook and Levin). Hardest in NP - if you solve one, you solve all NP problems.

acronyms.usage: Machine with exactly one transition per state per input symbol
Machine with zero or more transitions per state per input symbol
Finite automaton with stack memory for recognizing CFLs
Most powerful computational model, defines computability
Grammar with single non-terminal on LHS of productions
Language generated by CFG, recognized by PDA
Language recognized by Turing machine (Type 0 in Chomsky)
Language recognized by Linear Bounded Automaton (Type 1)

visualTips.visualization: Draw circles for states, arrows for transitions labeled with input symbols. Start state has incoming arrow, accept states have double circles.
Draw as: Left infinite tape divided into cells, read-write head above current cell, finite control box with current state inside. Head moves left/right, reads/writes symbols.
Draw pyramid with Type 3 (Regular) at top (smallest), Type 2 below, Type 1 below, Type 0 at bottom (largest). Shows containment: smaller on top = subset of larger below.
Draw two circles: P inside NP (P ⊆ NP). NP-complete as a smaller circle inside NP touching the boundary. NP-hard outside NP (larger area). Arrow: if P∩NPC ≠ ∅, then P = NP.
Draw stack as vertical array. Push: add symbol on top (like stacking plates). Pop: remove from top. Top of stack is where operations happen.

analogies.analogy: Like a light switch with preset patterns - limited memory, can only follow predetermined sequences. Cannot count beyond a fixed number.
Like a person with a notepad - can write down and later check information in LIFO order (like checking if parentheses are balanced).
Like a person with unlimited paper and time - can solve any algorithmic problem given enough resources. The ultimate general-purpose computer.
P = Finding a route on map (can compute quickly), NP = Verifying a treasure map (once found, easy to verify). The question is: are finding and verifying equally hard?
Like having master keys - if you can open one lock, you can open all locks in the building (solve one NPC, solve all NP).
Like trying to predict your own future - a program cannot analyze itself completely without running (and potentially going infinite).
